summary:
The Kunda culture was a Mesolithic hunter-gatherer-fisher tradition of the eastern Baltic region — encompassing modern Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, and parts of Finland, Russia, and Belarus — dated to approximately 8500–5000 BCE. Named after the town of Kunda in northern Estonia, the culture is identified by its bone and antler artifact repertoire, including harpoons, fishhooks, and elaborately decorated tools, alongside a stone tool industry of microliths and blades.

Kunda communities inhabited the lakeshores, river banks, and coastal zones of the eastern Baltic following the retreat of the ice sheets and the establishment of the Baltic basin's complex postglacial hydrological history. They were skilled fishers and hunters, exploiting the rich aquatic resources of the Baltic, inland lakes, and rivers — elk, beaver, wild pig, seal, and fish all feature in Kunda site faunal assemblages. The organic richness of many Kunda sites, preserved by waterlogged conditions, has yielded important collections of worked bone, antler, wood, and bark artifacts rarely preserved in drier sites. The Kunda culture belongs to a broader family of forest-zone Mesolithic cultures around the eastern Baltic and contributes to understanding the diversity of hunter-gatherer lifeways in the forests and waterways of postglacial northern Europe.

images.caption: Photo of tools used by Kunda Culture at The Estonian History Museum
